creator.name: Jason Yadlovski
creator.niche: DaVinci Resolve, especially audio and Fairlight
creator.oneLiner: Learn DaVinci Resolve from 31 videos with Jason's hands-on approach.
suggestedQuestions: How do I properly set audio levels so YouTube doesn't compress my videos?
What's the best compressor ratio and reduction for dialogue in DaVinci Resolve?
How do I use the new AI speech generator tool in Resolve 21?
What should I do before updating to DaVinci Resolve 21 to avoid losing my project?

voiceProfile.styleSummary: Jason speaks with casual, conversational clarity and methodical pacing. He breaks down technical DaVinci Resolve features into digestible steps while maintaining an approachable, non-intimidating tone that addresses his core audience of creators learning the software.
voiceProfile.rhythm: Mix of medium-length sentences with frequent short procedural statements ("I'm going to click here", "Let's talk about it"), creating a measured, step-by-step cadence that mirrors hands-on tutorial instruction rather than rapid-fire delivery.

If you've ever [problem], that's what we're going to be talking about today.
So, you've got [scenario] and you want to [goal], right?
You can now [capability] here in DaVinci Resolve 21 automatically.
closings: So with that said, I will see you in the next video. Peace.
With that said, I'm off to [action] for you guys, and I will see you in the next video. Peace.
I'll see you in the next one. Peace.
Thanks so much for watching, and I'll catch you in the next video.
catchphrases: I'm going to show you how
I'm going to go ahead and
voiceProfile.vocabulary: Mid-register technical vocabulary with accessible explanations; uses software jargon (keyframes, LUTs, Fusion, color grading) but explains terms without condescension; occasional filler ('um', 'so', 'like') keeps tone conversational rather than polished.
quirks: Self-identifies repeatedly as 'an audio guy' to establish credibility and personality
Uses rhetorical questions to invite viewer participation ('right?', 'What do you guys think?')
Often expresses skepticism or curiosity about his own discoveries ('I didn't know that', 'that surprised me', 'honestly')
Addresses audience directly as 'you guys' and invites feedback throughout, creating collaborative learning dynamic
Frames features pragmatically: 'it could come in really handy', 'I would use this'
exemplars: Let's talk about it and I'm going to show you how to use it.
I help you edit faster in DaVinci Resolve.
Honestly, the results are pretty impressive, but you be the judge here.
So with that said, I will see you in the next video. Peace.
Now, there's tons of third party ones, but in this case, I'm going to use the one built into Resolve.
I'm going to make some more videos for you guys, and I will see you in the next video.
If you're interested, got any questions, leave a comment down below, I'm happy to answer them.
I'm just a regular Joe like you guys trying out the professional monitor.

chunks.text: In DaVinci Resolve 21, we have the new speech generator tool. >> [music] >> Let's talk about it and I'm going to show you how to use it. So, inside of DaVinci Resolve here, I'm currently in the edit tab and we're going to use the new speech generator. So, I'm going to come up to timeline. I'm going to come down to AI tools and we have speech generator. When you first open up the speech generator, this is what the window looks like. And now we're going to be able to put our text right in this box right here, use a voice model, and then have that text turned into spoken word. And Resolve's going to do all that for us. So, I have some words here that I already came up with. So, I'm just paste that into my text box here. And now we can use one of several different voice models that are already included in Resolve. We have two different male voices and two different female voices. But, what if I wanted to be my own voice? I can do that super easily. I'm going to come to custom voice. And then right here, I'm going to want to select a file that I can use that is of me that Resolve's going to use that as a sample, learn my voice, and then use that as a model to turn the text into speech that sounds like me. So, I'm going to go ahead and click load so I can select a wave file. And if I jump on my hard drive here, I already recorded and exported out a sample. So, that's this right here and it does need to be in a wave format. It doesn't seem like the AIFF files or the MP 3 files, those don't work. It needs to be a wave file. So, I have this right here. I'm going to say open. And then once you've got that in there, it's going to use that audio as a sample to learn your voice or whatever voice you might want to use and then be able to turn our text into spoken word. So, down here you can change your settings if you want. In this case, I'm just going to leave everything as it is. I'm just going to leave the file name as default name for now. Audio track, you can select which one you want. I'm going to say audio track one. And then I'm going to go ahead and hit generate. Well, what you can do is take a snippet of their voice, have DaVinci Resolve learn their voice, so it creates a model of it, and then you can just input the text however you want, with whatever you want, saying whatever you want, and then run the speech generator here, and it's going to automatically use their voice and whatever text you put in there to recreate new dialogue, or new speech for whatever you might need it for. chunks.text: If you've ever recorded audio and you don't know how to fix it because it just doesn't sound right, that's what we're going to be talking about today. My name is Jason and I help you learn Dinci Resolve so that you can edit faster, sound better, cuz I'm an audio guy, and just make it a little less intimidating when you're working in Dinci Resolve. So, today we're taking a look at a bunch of sample files that one of our viewers had sent in, and he was just having trouble figuring out like, what's going on with my audio? why doesn't it sound good when I use a preset from uh something like uh Cap Cut, I think it was, he said, and I can't figure out what's going on. I just don't understand audio enough. Could you take a look at it? So, I said, "Sure, let's take a look at it." And I figured I would just record it as I look through it here. We'll talk through it so that you can see how I would approach a problem like this. So, he records uh videos for I guess a Swedish audience, I think he said, and it's uh short form videos about math. So, he wanted to kind of get some better videos going. So, he upgraded his microphone, got a new microphone, and it really didn't sound as good as he was hoping. So, it sounds like there's a little too much low-end in it. And um let's just jump into Dinci Resolve here and we're going to take a look at it and see if we can make it sound better for him. chunks.text: You can now animate anything to audio here in DaVinci Resolve 21 automatically. No plugins, no key frames, you could do it right inside of DaVinci Resolve 21. And the movement you're seeing now is driven directly by the audio. Let's jump in Resolve. I'm going to show you how to do it. In DaVinci Resolve 21, currently in the beta version here, I've got a Fusion composition where I've created just this little thing that you see on the screen here, right? Four DaVinci Resolve logos and some text. And I wanted to react to this song that I have down here in my timeline. Now, we used to be able to do it, we had to use extra plugins or just took a whole lot of work. Now, it's really easy and we can do it right in Fusion. So, I'm going to select my Fusion clip and jump over into Fusion. In the Fusion page here, I created this little graphic just very simply with a few logos right here, a transform to make the logos the size that I want. I merge those all together using a multi-merge node. And then I have some text here for DaVinci Resolve 21. They all connect together in a merge node, and then I have a transform node after all that, and that's where the magic's going to happen. Now, if I select this transform node right here, you see if I change the size, they all move together, and that's the effect I was going for with this particular graphic. So, in order to have the whole graphic here react to the music that we have in the edit page, here's how we're going to do that. I'm going to use this transform node. I'm going to come up into my inspector right up here, and I want to use the size to change as the beat of the music happens. So, I'm just going to right-click on size. I'm going to come down to modify with and Fairlight Animator. That's the new tool here that allows something or any parameter, almost any parameter, to react to a piece of audio. Um, when it comes to inputs on the back of the monitor, you've got a bunch of different inputs. So, you've got a USB-C, one of those. You've got two 2.1 HDMI ports. We have a DisplayPort 1.4, and we've got an RJ45 jack. Now, you might be thinking, "Why do I need that RJ45 jack? What am I hooking up to Ethernet for my monitor?" Well, if you work in a studio environment where you have multiple monitors of these, they

chunks.text: Should you upgrade to DaVinci Resolve 21? Honestly, maybe not. And even though it's not out quite yet, I've got five things that you want to think about before you go ahead and make that upgrade as soon as it drops. Number one here, the mid-project warning. So, if you are in the middle of a project, you're working through something that's important, or even if it's just a YouTube video, you probably want to wait until the project is done before you go ahead and update to DaVinci Resolve 21 once it comes out. I know for me, that's what I do. I never update in the middle of a project because I can introduce problems, things might not work, get thrown out of whack, and you don't want to do that, especially if you're working for paying clients. >> [groaning] >> Finish that project first, back up everything in DaVinci Resolve, got a video about that up here, and then go ahead and update to the new version. The second thing to think about here is if you use a lot of different kinds of plugins. Sometimes when we get a new version of software, the plugins that we use all the time need to be updated as well, or they may not work exactly how we remember them working. And that can cause a problem when you're working on your projects. You don't want to get stuck where you don't have the tools that you normally use to be able to finish up your project. So, that's something that you want to check before you upgrade. Make sure any kind of plugins or anything that you use is compatible with the new DaVinci Resolve 21 update when it drops. The third thing to think about here is whether or not you actually need any of those new features. If you don't need any of the new features that are coming out, which honestly, I don't know what they are yet, but but as soon as it comes out, I'm going to dive into it. But if you don't need them, don't worry about upgrading right away. Keep doing what you're doing, let things happen with the new release, let people try it out and see how it goes. And then when you're ready, you can back up your stuff and go ahead and upgrade. If you do need some of those new features, whatever they might be, then go ahead back up your stuff and maybe then it is time for you

chunks.text: upgrading right away. Keep doing what you're doing, let things happen with the new release, let people try it out and see how it goes. And then when you're ready, you can back up your stuff and go ahead and upgrade. If you do need some of those new features, whatever they might be, then go ahead back up your stuff and maybe then it is time for you to upgrade. But if you're not too worried about the new stuff, then just give it some time and stick with the version that you're on. The fourth thing to think about here is stability, right? A software that's new inevitably has some bugs in it. Now, I have to be honest, every time I've updated Resolve, even to beta versions, I've never had a problem. Well, that's not true. I've not never had a problem. A long time ago, like in Resolve 15, I might have had some problems, but in the last few versions, I've had no issues at all. I work on a Mac. I find that on the PC side, there's a little bit more things that could go wrong or or problems that you might run into, but on the Mac end, you're pretty much good to go as soon as even a beta comes out. But that's something that you want to think about. Make sure it's stable. Sometimes you might want to wait a little bit so that everybody else can test it out and then you upgrade. And I do that with other softwares, but part of my job here is to inform you about DaVinci Resolve. So, I always upgrade right away, but I do make sure that everything's backed up before I upgrade. So, the fifth thing here, when should you actually upgrade? Well, like we talked about, if you need some of those new features, whatever they might be, that's probably a good time to upgrade as long as you're not in the middle of a project cuz you don't want to upgrade in the middle of a project. Maybe you've waited a few weeks or a month and you've seen some of the bugs that have been found from other users, and you know that Blackmagic Design is pretty good about fixing those things and then sending out updates to us. So, if you see that they've sent out, you know, some updates, 21.1 or .05, 21.05, whatever, it's probably an okay time to update because they fixed some of those bugs that people are commonly running

chunks.text: that have been found from other users, and you know that Blackmagic Design is pretty good about fixing those things and then sending out updates to us. But Blackmagic Design is really good at taking care of problems for us pretty quickly. Another way that you can check and make sure that it's stable is just look around the community a little bit. You're going to have the whole database with all of the projects in there. All of my projects are now backed up. I'm going to hit okay. I can just click back on my project libraries here and if I take a look in finder, here is my backed up database. It's only 1.7 gigs and that is several years worth of YouTube videos posting

Most important thing, back up that project library so you don't lose any of your projects. Now, if you want an extra layer of security, you can export out individual projects. Here's how you do that. You could right click on it and then you can export the project and that's going to export just the DaVinci Resolve project file. It's not going to include any of your media. So, that's important to know because we also have export project archive and that will include all of your media, all of your assets, everything for your project. So, if all the media and assets in your projects is 100 gigs, that folder is going to be 100 gigs versus if you export just the project, that's only the DaVinci Resolve project file and it's going to be a very small file, but it doesn't include any of your media files. So, I would recommend this for any of those important projects. If you want a full backup of everything in the project, you export the project archive and if you want just the actual DaVinci Resolve project, maybe you want to put it in the folder, you go ahead and export that project and then you can put it wherever you want. Next, here's one that a lot of people don't think about and that is backing up your power bins. In Resolve, I'm in the edit tab and I've got the media pool open and I've got my power bins right here. chunks.text: If your editing feels a little slow, it's [music] probably not your computer. It's these five mistakes. You fix these and you'll be editing faster today. Mistake number one, you are clicking [music] everything. If you are doing this, you're clicking the razor, then you're clicking where you want to make your cuts, and then you're clicking the arrow, clicking a clip, right-clicking, delete selected, then you're grabbing your clips, moving it over. You are wasting a ton of time. You need to be using some keyboard shortcuts. Here's just a few of the keyboard shortcuts that you should be using. The J, K, and L keys are going to help you navigate around your timeline. L is going to go forward. Double-click, it's going to go forward even faster. K is going to stop it. J is going to go backwards. If you press it multiple times, you go backwards even faster. And if you hold the letter K and press either J or L, you're going to go forwards frame by frame for L and backwards frame by frame with K. Cut key, which for me I remapped to S, and then the cut and ripple delete keys. So, if I come ahead, I'm going to press my Q key, which I've remapped, it's going to make a cut and get rid of the gap. And if I wanted it to go the other way, I've remapped the W key. You can see the default on the screen here. It's going to make a cut and ripple delete the other way. Control Z is one that you'll use all the time to undo. If you are trying out different clips in a spot on your timeline, the letter D comes in super handy because it's going to enable or disable a clip for you. Another keyboard shortcut that comes in really handy is the paste attributes. So, you would just come to one clip, and if you want to copy those attributes, select the clip, command or control C to copy it, go to your new clip, and use option on a Mac or alt on a PC and the letter V, and that's going to bring up all these different attributes that you can paste from one clip to the next. In this case, I want to do what's selected. I'm going to hit apply. And now this clip matches the previous clip that we copied from. Easy to copy and paste attributes. Saves a ton of time. Mistake number two here is that you rebuild everything in
chunks.videoTitle: 5 Mistakes Slowing Down Your Editing (Fix These Now)

chunks.text: these different attributes that you can paste from one clip to the next. In this case, I want to do what's selected. I'm going to hit apply. And now this clip matches the previous clip that we copied from. Easy to copy and paste attributes. Saves a ton of time. Mistake number two here is that you rebuild everything in every project. You're not using the tools available to you in Resolve here like presets, power grades, and power bins. In the media pool here, you can have your power bins, which allow you to put things in that power bin, and they will be available in every single project. If you don't see your power bins right here, come to the three little dots, and make sure power bins is turned on here. You can see I've got power bins for all kinds of music, sound effects, different brand assets. I even have some zoom in and zoom out, some talking head zooms, so I don't have to make them over and over again. I've got overlays for all kinds of different things, different types of text effects that I like, even a couple things from my class that I like to include in some of my videos. And these will appear in every single project. The next big one is power grades. If you film in a similar environment or you've got a color grade that you like and you want to keep it, but you don't want to recreate it every single project, here's what you do. You jump over into the color tab, and you want to open up your gallery, and if you don't see these over here, we have stills, power grades, and timelines. You want to click on this little icon right here, and power grades are going to be available to you in every single project. And it's as easy as clicking the one that you want and dropping it right onto your image. And how do you create that power grade? Simple. Let's say I take this and I make a little adjustment. All you have to do is come to your image right here, right-click, and say grab a still. And as long as you're selected on your power grade bin over here, it's going to put it right in there for you. If you were on stills right here, it's going to put it in this bin, and that's only going to be available in this project. So, you want to make sure that you use power grades.
chunks.videoTitle: 5 Mistakes Slowing Down Your Editing (Fix These Now)

chunks.text: to your image right here, right-click, and say grab a still. And as long as you're selected on your power grade bin over here, it's going to put it right in there for you. chunks.text: When your audio sounds bad, you probably use words like this to describe it. Muddy, harsh, honky, thin. But what do these words actually mean? What do they sound like? chunks.text: If your timeline ever looks like this, maybe even way worse, with clips stacked all on top of each other, and you're feeling like I might be losing a little control, here's a quick way to clean it up instantly. Hey, I'm Jason and I help creators just like you learn Da Vinci Resolve so that you can edit faster, sound better, cuz I'm an audio guy, and edit with confidence using Da Vinci Resolve. Today, we're going to take [music] a look at something called compound clips, and it's going to help keep your timeline clean and easy to manage. Let's check it out. So, in Resolve, here I am in the edit page, and you can see here I've got a whole bunch of different clips stacked up. In this case, it's just all the different icons I created for uh Da Vinci Resolve here. And let's say maybe I wanted to like fade these out all at once. Well, I don't necessarily want to have to come in here, come into my timeline, you know, fade all these in and out. Or maybe I want to apply an effect to them. I don't want to do it to all of them cuz it just takes a lot of time. That's where something like compound clips can come in handy. And to create a compound clip, all I have to do is select all of the clips that I want to include in that. And they could be anywhere in the timeline. It doesn't matter. They don't have to be right on top of each other. I'm going to rightclick, say new compound clip. It's going to ask me for a name. I'm going to call it DR logos, and hit create. So now you're going to see it appear as one clip on my timeline. But we still have all of those layers in there. And if we come to my media pool here, open that up in my master bin. We can see that we've got a compound clip right here called DR logos. So if I wanted to use this clip again, I could just drag it in the timeline and there's all my logos perfectly fine. Now they can be animated. You can do whatever you want to them before you make the compound clip and save it as a compound clip. Use it over and over again. Now, a little bonus tip. You're like, "What if I want to change something in there?" You've got a few options here. So, if we wanted to open this clip back up and just not have that compound clip anymore, I can

Now, if I undo that, an even better way might be to select the clip, right click, and say open in timeline. So, when I do that, now what happens is I'm inside of that compound clip. chunks.text: If you've ever adjusted your audio by feel, maybe you turn down your music a little bit, you turn up the dialogue a little bit, and then you wonder, is this even right? You're not alone. Audio levels are one of the biggest guessing games in video editing. How loud should my dialogue be? How quiet should my music be? Should my sound effects stand out? Should they blend in? And the truth is, there are guidelines that you want to follow, and they [music] don't change based on the software that you're editing in. In this video, I'm going to walk you through proper audio levels for [music] video editors. We're going to talk about dialogue, music, sound effects, and how they all work together. And these principles apply whether you're using Da Vinci Resolve, Premiere Pro, Final Cut, Cap Cut, it doesn't matter. It's the same settings that you want to meet in any [music] program that you're editing in. I'll be demonstrating this inside of Da Vinci Resolve. And you can think of it as an example. So, if you're using Premiere Pro, Final Cut, whatever it might be, try and match the same levels that I'm getting in Da Vinci Resolve using the software that you're editing in. We're also going to talk about how individual clips fit into the bigger picture of all of our audio, including mixing, [music] loudness, and lus, which is also part of mastering. All so that you can understand how everything connects together. By the end of this video, you'll stop guessing and you'll be confident in your audio levels, no matter [music] what program you're editing in. So, let's jump into it. So, as we get started here working with our audio levels, I have a mini documentary that's already edited together, and we've got a lot of different things happening here. We've got different kinds of dialogue. We've got music in here and we've got sound effects. So, the first step of the process is to get organized. When you're working with your audio, no matter what kind of project you're working on, you want to separate things out into different tracks. And this is just going to help you keep things organized and work through all of your audio a little bit easier. So, in the project here, I've already got four different tracks

And I separated them out by the microphone type. Now, I'm the voice in each one of these, but if there was different voices, I would separate each voice into a different track. And then separated by location. So, I've got different microphones, different locations, and then I'm the only one speaking, so I've separated it out into four different tracks.
